food_subgroup: Cooked fish rice bowl with egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for salmon donburi with egg; closest_parent 'cooked fish dish' (FOODON_00002809) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked salmon as the primary ingredient; no composite donburi-with-egg code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for salmon rice bowl with egg composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 150 g cooked salmon, 200 g steamed white rice, one medium egg (~50 g), and sauce/garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked (FDC ID 175168); Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169760); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424); soy sauce contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Salmon Don with Egg in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values estimated as a weighted composite: 37.5% cooked salmon, 50% cooked white rice, 12.5% hard-boiled egg, plus minor soy sauce contribution, normalised to per-100 g basis. Weights reflect typical 400 g bowl composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice (short-grain, cooked) GI ~54 used as primary glycemic driver; salmon and egg contribute negligible glycemic load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 54 assigned based on short-grain cooked white rice (GI ~54, University of Sydney GI Database). Salmon and egg have negligible carbohydrate content and do not materially alter the composite GI. GL per serving = (54 × 71.2 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 38.4, rounded to 38.5. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy-based sauce (~210 mg/100 g; ~840 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit sauce quantity, as a full serving can contribute approximately 840 mg sodium — roughly 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice (GL ~38.5 per serving) despite low-GI classification
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the large rice portion produces a substantial glycemic load (~38.5 per serving); substituting brown rice or reducing portion size to 150 g rice can significantly lower postprandial glucose response while retaining the protein benefit of salmon and egg.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from Atlantic salmon (EPA and DHA) with moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Atlantic salmon is a rich source of cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA); regular consumption as part of a balanced diet is consistent with cardiovascular disease prevention guidelines, provided soy sauce is used sparingly to manage sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon Don with Egg is a Japanese-influenced rice bowl dish popular in Singapore's casual dining and food delivery scene. It consists of a generous portion of cooked Atlantic salmon — typically pan-seared, grilled, or lightly torched — placed over a bed of steamed short-grain white rice, topped with a soft-boiled, poached, or onsen-style egg. The dish is finished with a savoury soy-based sauce, often incorporating mirin, sake, or sesame oil, and may be garnished with spring onions, furikake, or nori strips. The flavour profile is rich, umami-forward, and mildly sweet, with the creamy yolk of the egg adding textural contrast to the flaky salmon. Nutritionally, the dish provides high-quality protein from both salmon and egg, heart-healthy omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from the salmon, and sustained energy from the rice base. The primary clinical considerations are the moderate-to-high glycemic load from the white rice portion and the sodium contribution from soy-based sauces, making portion control and sauce moderation important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. Per 100 g, the dish delivers approximately 148 kcal, 10.2 g protein, 4.1 g fat, and 17.8 g carbohydrate. It is dairy-free and suitable for pescatarians, though it contains fish, egg, soy, and sesame allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in salmon don with egg?
Salmon don with egg protein content
Is salmon don good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Salmon Don with Egg found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked Atlantic salmon, cooked short-grain white rice, and hard-boiled egg, normalised to per-100 g. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database data for short-grain white rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (cooked Atlantic salmon FDC 175168, cooked short-grain white rice FDC 169760, hard-boiled egg FDC 173424) weighted at 37.5%, 50%, and 12.5% respectively to reflect a typical 400 g bowl. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for these ingredients; no industrial trans fats present. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 1.5 estimated from USDA salmon and egg data). 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on short-grain cooked white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category set to 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 5. Halal status is marked false because the dish as served at The Rainbow Bistro has not been confirmed halal-certified; salmon itself is permissible but egg and sauce sourcing are unverified. 6. Cuisine tags in the input (Malaysian, Curry Rice, Nasi Lemak) appear to be platform-level category tags unrelated to this specific dish; the dish is Japanese-influenced fusion and has been reclassified accordingly. 7. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA composite estimation for this dish type and are documented as such.
